title: Beachcroft, La Cache du Bourg, St. Clement: Planning Application (P/2024/1334): Appeal Decision
text:
A decision made 14 April 2026:Decision Reference: MD-ENV-2026-410Public Subject: P/2024/1334 (Beachcroft, La Cache du Bourg, St. Clement) Report Title: Subject: Planning appeal decision: P/2024/1334 (Beachcroft, La Cache du Bourg, St. Clement)Public Decision(s): The Minister decided to dismiss the appeal, and to refuse planning permission (P/2024/1334), subject to reason 2 being deleted from the decision notice, in accordance with the inspector’s recommendation.Reason for Decision(s):Having considered all aspects of the appeal the Minister decided to give effect to the inspector’s recommendation and was satisfied that there were no reasons not to do so.Resource Implications: There are no new financial and/or staffing implications arising as a consequence of this Ministerial Decision. Action Required: The Ministerial Office to advise the Judicial Greffe of the decision.Signature: Signed By: Minister for the EnvironmentDate Signed: Date of Decision (If different from Date Signed): 

 <h1

--- Inspector's Report ---

PLANNING AND BUILDING (JERSEY) LAW 2002 (as amended)
Appeal under Article 108 against a decision made to refuse
planning permission
REPORT TO THE MINISTER FOR THE ENVIRONMENT
By Mr Philip Staddon BSc, Dip, MBA, MRTPI
____________________________________________________
Appellant: Mr Nick Rothwell
Site address: Beachcroft, La Cache du Bourg, St. Clement, Jersey, JE2 6FX
Application reference number: P/2024/1334
Proposal: ‘Demolition of existing house. Construct replacement energy efficient
house with basement’ - 3D MODEL AVAILABLE
Decision Notice date: 16 October 2025
Procedure: Hearing held on 11 February 2026
Inspector’s site visit: 9 February 2026
Inspector’s report date: 7 April 2026
___________________________________________________________
INTRODUCTION
1. This report contains my assessment of the planning appeal made by
Mr Nick Rothwell (the appellant). The appeal is made against the decision of
the department for Infrastructure and the Environment (the planning
authority) to refuse to grant planning permission for a proposal to construct
a replacement dwelling at Beachcroft in St Clement.
PROCEDURAL MATTERS
2. Article 114(1) of the Law states that planning appeals against decisions to
refuse planning permission are to be considered by way of written
representations, although this does not apply if representations were
received at the application stage, which was the case for this application.
3. Whilst Article 114(2) does allow me the discretion to consult with the parties
on the use of the written representations route in such cases, I elected not
to do so in this case. This is because the planning decision was made by the
Planning Committee against officer recommendation and interested parties
have raised multiple objections at the application and appeal stage. As a
result, the Hearing process was most appropriate to examine these
complexities and ensure that all parties had the opportunity to be heard.
4. In the course of the application, amendments were made to the scheme. I
have focused my assessment on the revised proposal that was considered
1

and determined by the planning authority. I have been advised that the 3D
digital model was not updated to reflect the amended plans. I have
therefore not relied upon it for detailed assessment.
5. I have also noted concerns that the amendments to the scheme and new
document submissions may not have been publicised, although Mr Gladwin
(for the planning authority) advised that when a proposal is being reduced
in size through amendments, re-advertisement would not always be
undertaken. Given that near neighbours appeared to be aware of the
proposal and have actively participated in this appeal, I am satisfied that no
issues of unfairness arise in this case.
THE APPEAL SITE, THE APPEAL PROPOSAL AND THE APPLICATION
DETERMINATION
The appeal site
6. Beachcroft is a detached 2 storey 3-bedroom dwelling located on the south
side of La Cache du Bourg, which is a narrow private road that links to La
Grande Route de la Cote, a short distance to the east of the site.
7. The main house structure sits in the middle of its modest rectangular
shaped plot, with a single storey garage wing extending north towards the
private road. The side walls of the house are inset a little in from the plot
boundaries creating narrow paths either side. The rear of the house faces
the coast, and there is direct access to the beach from a gate at the bottom
of the plot.
8. The rear garden contains a swimming pool and hard surfaced areas,
enclosed by boundary walls, which are about head height, close to the
house and step down a little in height further into the garden. The house
dates from the 1980s1 and comprises rendered walls with stone quoin
features, and a pitched tiled roof. The rear beach facing elevation is
extensively glazed, and includes a ground floor conservatory and a first-
floor balcony, which spans most of the width of the house.
9. The dwellings located along La Cache du Bourg form a row all enjoying
southerly views to the beach. Most are detached, but they are quite closely
spaced with limited separating gaps between.
10. There is a variety of architectural styles, although most are two storey
pitched roof dwellings of traditional and conventional post war designs.
There are a few modern flat roof design dwellings in the vicinity, notably
Greta (on a narrow plot) and Beach House2 (on a large plot by the slipway),
both located further to the east of the appeal site. There are also examples
of properties that have been remodelled and refurbished, including Landfall,
which is the neighbouring property to the west of the appeal site.
1 There are different dates quoted by the parties in their written submissions. The applicant’s Design and Access
Statement refer to the house construction ‘circa 1980’, whereas the case made by MS Planning (25 November
2025) on behalf of an interested party states that the property ‘dates from 1989’.
2 Formerly Villa Paloma.
2

11. The appeal site and the surrounding residential development lies within the
Built-up Area (BUA) as defined within the Bridging Island Plan (BIP)
(adopted March 2022) proposals map. The site also falls within the defined
Local Centre (Greve D’Azette La Rocque).
The P/2024/1334 proposal
12. The proposal seeks permission to demolish the existing dwelling and
redevelop a new 4-bedroom house, which the appellant describes as ‘energy
efficient’. It would include a basement and 3 levels of above ground
accommodation.
13. The basement would have an internal floor area of 99.323 square metres
and would include a garage for a car (accessed via a car lift) and bicycles, a
cinema room, a server room, plant and storage rooms, and a hall area, with
a lift which would rise to the all 3 floors above).
14. The ground floor would have an internal area of 87.354 square metres and
would include an entrance hall, WCs and the main living area in the form of
an open plan lounge/kitchen, with glazed doors opening to the garden which
would include a new pool. The first floor (95.98 square metres internally)
would include 3 bedrooms with en suites and balconies. The top floor would
be set back somewhat with a smaller footprint of 49.70 square metres
internally; this would accommodate a bedroom, en suite and a seaward
facing balcony (22.45 square metres).
15. The house would be of a modern design, with extensive glazing and balcony
features on its front and rear elevations. It would have a flat roof structure,
with some rooftop mounted solar panels, which would rise marginally above
the ridge height of the existing house. The proposed materials include
marble cladding, grey rain screen cladding, and white render.
The application consideration and determination
16. The application was submitted in October 2024, and appeared to have a
protracted assessment process, during which amendments were made and
further material submitted. It was reported to the 25 September 2025
Planning Committee meeting for a determination. Whilst the case officer had
recommended granting planning permission, the Committee did not agree,
and resolved that it was minded to refuse the planning application. Under
the ‘cool off’ procedure, the proposal was considered again at the 16
October 2025 meeting. The Planning Committee then resolved to refuse the
application.
17. The Decision Notice cites 2 reasons for refusing to grant planning
permission:
1. Insufficient information has been submitted to demonstrate
demonstrable aesthetic or practical benefit to justify replacement rather
3 Dimensions taken from drawing No 101/Rev B
4 Not including the garage area which is notated as 19.58 square metres internally
3

than re-furbishment. The proposal is therefore contrary to Policies SP1
and GD5 of the Bridging Island Plan, 2022.
2. The proposed development by virtue of its overall height and proximity
to the boundaries would cause an unreasonable overbearing and
overlooking impact on the amenities of neighbouring properties
contrary to the Policy GD1 of the Bridging Island Plan 2022.
18. The appellant’s appeal is made against this decision.
SUMMARY OF THE APPELLANT’S GROUNDS OF APPEAL
19. The appellant’s case is set out in the appeal form and expanded upon in a
Statement of Case with appendices, and a Further Comments document.
The grounds stated in the appeal form are:
Ground 1 - Sufficient information has been submitted to demonstrate the
aesthetic and/or practical benefit to justify replacement rather than
refurbishment of the existing dwelling, to the satisfaction of policies SP1 and
GD5 of the Bridging Island Plan, 2022.
Ground 2 - The proposed development by virtue of its overall height and
proximity to the boundaries would not cause an unreasonable overbearing
or overlooking impact on the amenities of neighbouring properties. The
proposed development satisfies the requirements of policy GD1 of the
Bridging Island Plan 2022.
Ground 3 - When viewed in the round, the application satisfies the
requirements of the Bridging Island Plan 2022 and associated SPGs.
20. The Statement of Case concludes: The site is located in the built-up area
where the island plan directs development. The proposal will replace an
existing dwelling, within a private residential road, with a contemporary,
energy efficient dwelling. The proposal is supported by all the Government’s
consultees, except the policy team, who differ on how policy GD5 is
engaged. The proposal meets all the adopted space and parking standards.
At delegated officer level the application was supported, and we urge the
inspector and minister to find in favour of this appeal and overturn the
decision to refuse.
21. The Final Comments document provides responses and rebuttals to the case
made by the Department and to comments made by interested parties. It
addresses concerns about the proposed building's height, massing,
footprint, and impact on the site's character, emphasizing compliance with
policies and mitigating measures such as improved energy efficiency,
drainage solutions, and parking arrangements. The appellant disputes
claims of significant impact on neighbouring properties and highlights
amendments made during the application process to address concerns.
22. At the Hearing, the appellant’s case was made by Ms Duffell (planning
consultant), with contributions from the appellant.
4

THE PLANNING AUTHORITY’S RESPONSE
23. The planning authority’s case is set out in a Response document with
appendices, which include the officer report, the ‘cool off’ report, the
Committee minutes, and the Decision Notice.
24. The Response acknowledges that the application was recommended for
approval by the Infrastructure and Environment Department, but points out
that this does not make the decision unreasonable.
25. With regard to the first ground of appeal, it explains the Committee’s
concerns about the lack of justification for demolition over refurbishment,
and that the Committee strongly disagreed that the appellant had
demonstrated aesthetic and practical benefit to justify replacement over
refurbishment.
26. Concerning the second reason for refusal, the Response sets out that the
Committee considered the overall height and massing of the building and its
proximity to the party boundaries were unreasonable. It says that the site is
a constrained long narrow plot with neighbouring properties in close
proximity on both sides. It acknowledges that the site has more open
aspects to the north and particularly to the south to the beach and sea, but
the height of the building and proximity to the side boundaries, would in the
Committee’s opinion cause unreasonable overbearing and overlooking harm,
contrary to policy GD1.
27. It further explains that, after deliberation, including site visits and hearing
from both the appellant and objectors, the Committee unanimously refused
the application, citing insufficient evidence of aesthetic or practical benefits
for demolition and concerns about the height and proximity of the proposed
building to neighbouring boundaries, which is shown clearly on drawing
103A. The decision to refuse permission was made in accordance with
Policies SP1, GD1, and GD5 of the BIP.
28. With regard to the third ground, the Response explains that the Planning
Committee made the decision having taken account of all representations
received and all material considerations, and that it was entitled to come to
a different conclusion from the Department’s officer, and that the decision
to refuse the application was entirely reasonable.
29. At the Hearing, the planning authority’s case was presented by Mr Gladwin
(appeals officer).
SUMMARY OF THE VIEWS OF INTERESTED PARTIES
30. At the application stage, 12 representations were lodged, with a number
being from the same household. These included a wide range of objections
concerning alleged overdevelopment, inappropriate design and amenity
implications. I have reviewed and considered all of these representations in
making my assessment.
31. At the appeal stage, the following interested party submissions were made:
5

Mrs Simone Fosse – La Greve
32. Ms Fosse expresses disappointment that the application has gone to appeal
after being unanimously rejected by the Committee. She shares the
Committee’s view and challenges the claim that the existing house has
structural defects, noting the lack of supporting evidence. She submits that
the proposed dwelling's size and placement would have an unreasonable
impact on neighbouring properties, particularly her own, highlighting loss of
light and shadowing impacts. She also expresses concerns about the
excavation for the proposed basement, due to the large amount of waste
material generated and potential damage to her property and neighbouring
properties.
33. In addition to her personally submitted representations, MS Planning
submitted a detailed submission (17 pages plus appendices) and a further
comments document on her behalf. It contends that the application fails to
meet the criteria of policy GD5, as the evidence provided does not
demonstrate that refurbishment is unviable or that the replacement offers
significant aesthetic or practical benefits. Additionally, the proposed
development is deemed too large for the site, causing overbearing and
privacy issues for neighbours. The statement also highlights concerns about
the lack of compliance with other policies, including GD6 (design quality),
TT1 (safe travel), WER6 (surface water drainage), and NE1 (biodiversity
protection). The interested party concludes that the application does not
align with the Island Plan and lacks sufficient justification for approval.
34. At the Hearing, Mrs Fosse and Mr John Nicholson (planning consultant) both
attended and presented her case.
Mrs Joan Barrett – Landfall
35. Mrs Barrett, as an immediate neighbour, submits her strong objections on a
number of grounds. These include that the existing building is structurally
sound, and no structural survey or professional report has been provided to
justify demolition; environmental concerns emphasizing the importance of
reuse and recycling; that the proposed new building would significantly alter
the footprint and massing, extending further south towards the shoreline
and north towards the lane, which would change the site's character and its
relationship with neighbouring properties and the coastal setting; and that
there are concerns about excavation related to the basement construction.
Mrs Barrett respectfully requests the Planning Inspector to uphold the
Committee's decision and dismiss the appeal.
36. Mrs Barrett attended and spoke at the Hearing.
6

INSPECTOR’S ASSESSMENT
Ground 1 – Demolition/redevelopment
37. An important main issue in this case relates to whether the proposed
demolition of Beachcroft and its replacement by a new dwelling is
acceptable in planning policy terms, most notably with regard to policy GD5,
which specifically addresses this matter.
38. The Minister will be aware that the issue of demolition and replacement of
buildings is a matter that Jersey planning policy makers have recognised as
important for some time. Indeed, it can be traced back over a number of
iterations of Island Plans and the policy challenge has always been to find
the right balance between the desirability of retaining and reusing existing
buildings, in terms of avoiding waste and making best use of their embodied
carbon, and wider sustainability and environmental benefits that might arise
from redevelopment.
39. Policy GD5 limits support for demolition and replacement to 3
circumstances: 1) where it is not appropriate in sustainability terms, and/or
economically viable, to repair or refurbish the buildings; 2) where the
proposed replacement building or part of a building represents a more
sustainable use of land having regard to the density of existing and
proposed development, overall carbon impact, waste generation, and the
use and performance of materials and services; or 3) where there exists a
demonstrable aesthetic and practical benefit to replace over refurbishment.
The policy also requires such proposals to be supported by sufficient
information to justify demolition.
40. In this case, the application was originally submitted with only a limited
‘demolition justification’5 which concluded that: ‘It is considered that the
retention of the original house and its refurbishment would result in a
poorer quality of development for the site. Plans to demolish Beachcroft
would allow a replacement, modern, energy efficient building to be
developed and allow a more efficient use of the site.’
41. I understand that it has been the planning authority’s practice in recent
years to consult the policy team6 on proposals relating to policy GD5, to
ensure a consistency of approach. The consultation response (December
2024) ran to 5 pages and reviewed the proposal against the GD5 test,
identifying lack of information and inadequate justification. Whilst its
content on the third test (demonstrable aesthetic and practical benefit)
seemed a little muddled, the overall conclusion was clear. It stated:
The application does not give sufficient enough evidence to meet GD5 policy
consideration. It does not thoroughly explore all options to retain, in-whole
or in-part, through refurbishment or adaptation the existing building. There
is no sustainability assessment, carbon cost analysis, material recycling, no
economic evidence as to the cost differences.
5 Contained on the sixth page of the Design and Access Statement dated September 2024
6 Housing, Environment and Placemaking
7

The need to demonstrate why demolition over retention is evidenced in this
case has not met requirements set out in GD5.
42. The appellant subsequently provided a more detailed GD5 Report dated
6 May 2025. However, the policy team was not reconsulted and did not
therefore review the report. At the Hearing, Mr Gladwin explained that the
case officer was entitled to form her own view on the GD5 matter and,
whilst I agree, the coverage in the Committee report is rather scant. Given
the importance of this policy in this case, I have set out my assessment
below which tracks through each element, albeit that I recognise that the
appellant’s case focuses primarily on the third test.
43. In terms of the GD5(1) test, the appellant’s evidence does not demonstrate
that it is not appropriate in sustainability terms, and/or economically viable,
to repair or refurbish the buildings. The house is relatively modern and
sound and, when I inspected it, appeared to show no signs of structural
faults or other issues that might make refurbishment and repair difficult.
The appellant’s GD5 report actually evidences that the retrofit and extension
option would be a lower cost per square metre (psm) compared to
demolition and new build (£3,281/psm compared to £3,779.90 psm). Whilst
I note the appellant’s submission7 that ‘the replacement house is larger with
improved aesthetics, improved internal space and improved layout,
consequently has the highest valuation for resale’ this does not equate to a
demonstration that it is not appropriate to repair or refurbish the property. I
am also mindful that other properties nearby have followed the
repair/refurbish model successfully. The GD5(1) test is not met.
44. Under the GD5(2) test there are a number of policy metrics to consider to
determine whether the replacement building or part of a building represents
a more sustainable use of land.
45. With regard to ‘density’, the existing situation is a 3-bedroom detached
house located on a relatively modest sized plot. Whilst the replacement
would deliver a larger and more spacious house with 4 bedrooms, there
would be no net change in the number of dwellinghouses on the land. I do
not therefore consider that there would be any discernible sustainability
benefits arising under the ‘density’ metric.
46. Concerning ‘overall carbon impact’, the appellant’s own GD5 Report
confirms that, ‘In respect of total carbon and square metre basis the retrofit
and retrofit / extension options have a lower carbon generation’. Whilst
acknowledging that the replacement building may have lower running costs
and a longer lifespan, the evidence before me indicates that a refurbishment
option performs better in terms of overall carbon impact.
47. Regarding waste generation, the GD5 Report confirms that the replacement
dwelling, which will include complete demolition of Beachcroft and
excavation to provide a basement, would produce the most waste compared
to refurbishment/retrofit options, although I have not seen comparative
figures. Concerning ‘the use and performance of materials and services’, I
7 Section 9.1 of the GD5 Report dated 6 May 2025.
8

have noted the contents of the GD5 Report, but it does not provide me with
any convincing evidence that materials and services choices and
specifications will deliver any notable sustainability benefits.
48. I assess that the GD5(2) test is not met.
49. This then takes us to the GD5(3) test, concerning whether there is a
demonstrable aesthetic and practical benefit to replace Beachcroft over its
refurbishment. For the appellant, Ms Duffell identified that the ‘or’ in the
policy construction meant that compliance with GD5(3) alone was sufficient
to satisfy the policy, and she further highlighted the use of the term
‘demonstrable’ rather than ‘exceptional’. She is correct in her observations
here, although I think some care is needed in terms of contextualising this
element of the policy which, in turn, informs how a decision maker should
calibrate ‘demonstrable’.
50. In terms of broader policy context, GD5 plays an important role within the
BIP’s wider development strategy and related policies, the thrust of which
will inevitably involve recycling of sites and buildings within the BUA. The
GD5 policy construction contains an implicit presumption that existing
buildings should, first and foremost, be retained and refurbished, and that
redevelopment is ‘only’ supported in 3 specified circumstances.
51. I have assessed above that the first and second tests have not been met.
Whilst Ms Duffell is right to say that a GD5(3) compliant scheme can stand
alone, that test does not exist in a vacuum. The failure of the proposal
under tests (1) and (2) is not without consequence. In essence, it points to
a form of development that does not score particularly well in terms of
sustainability, and policy SP1 (responding to climate change)
considerations.
52. The appeal proposal effectively precludes, and would occur at the expense
of, an economically viable and less carbon impacting refurbishment option.
That is not to say that the proposal is automatically unacceptable under
GD5(3), nor that any aesthetic or practical benefits have to reach the
‘exceptional’ bar. However, to my mind, ‘demonstrable’ in this context
means that the benefits need to be clear and convincing. Moreover, the
policy’s explanatory text8 states that under this test, ‘it must be
demonstrated that it would not be practically possible to achieve aesthetic
benefit through the retention and refurbishment of the existing building, or
parts of the existing building.’
53. In terms of aesthetic benefits of the appeal proposal, any judgement here
clearly involves a degree of subjectivity about the merits of the existing
house, what could be achieved by refurbishment, and what is proposed by
replacement. My starting point is that, whilst Beachcroft might be
unremarkable architecturally, and in need of some updating, it is not an
unpleasant or ugly building that shouts out for replacement. Indeed, its
relative ordinariness sits quite comfortably within the mix of different styles,
sizes and designs of properties along La Cache du Bourg. I note here that
8 Bridging Island Plan (March 2022) – page 91
9

the framing of GD5 presumes to protect a large stock of buildings, many of
which may be unremarkable or ordinary.
54. At the Hearing, Ms Duffell stated that it was ‘glaringly obvious that the end
product would be much better’. There is little doubt in my mind that, to
future residents, the proposed house would provide very agreeable, indeed
luxurious, accommodation with its spacious layout, large room sizes and
balconies, some of which would enjoy seaward views.
55. However, in terms of aesthetics when viewed from the public realm,
including the street and the beach, my assessment is less favourable. The
replacement dwelling would be much bigger and bulkier than the building it
would replace, and it would adopt a design which contrasts to that of its
immediate neighbours to the east, west and north. Adopting a different
modern design is not inappropriate in itself, but, in my judgement, the
design is strident and excessive for this modest sized plot. This is a matter
of judgement and it was clearly a matter identified by the case officer who
required size reductions in the building design. To my mind, the design still
seems to have exceeded the maximum capacity of the site, and would look
somewhat out of place and out of character, set between its two more
domestic scaled and more traditionally styled neighbouring dwellings. I am
therefore unconvinced that aesthetic benefits would be delivered.
56. Concerning the practical benefits, those cited by the appellant appear to
relate to matters of convenience and personal preference, and a desire to
maximise the space from the site, rather than compelling planning reasons.
57. Accepting a degree of subjectivity around the above matters, there is no
evidence before me to suggest that aesthetic, and indeed practical, benefits
could not be achieved through retention and refurbishment, as guided by
the GD5 companion text. In this regard, I have noted the appellant’s
concern that a retrofit will always be a ‘total compromise’, but there can be
no escaping the fact that the planning system, through the application of
the BIP policies, does enforce balances and compromises in the wider public
interest. Moreover, there are multiple examples of successful retention and
refurbishment schemes in Jersey, including in close proximity to the appeal
site.
58. Rounding up all of the above, I conclude that the proposal fails to satisfy
any of the GD5 tests that would allow for the demolition and replacement of
Beachcroft. As such, there is a fundamental conflict with policy GD5. There
is also a consequential conflict with policy SP1 which, in its measures to
support the response to climate change, includes support for the retention
and appropriate reuse and retrofitting of existing buildings. The ground 1
appeal should therefore fail.
Ground 2 – Impact of the amenities of neighbouring properties
59. The Planning Committee’s second reason alleges unreasonable overbearing
and overlooking effects, as a result of the proposed building’s overall height
and proximity to its boundaries. The reason does not identify the
neighbouring properties it considers to be affected, but there was
10

agreement between the main parties at the Hearing that it related to
Landfall (Mrs Barrett’s property to the west) and La Greve (Mrs Fosse’s
property to the east); there was a consensus that the property to the north,
Kate’s Cottage, would not experience unreasonable effects.
60. The primary policy concerning these matters is GD1, which covers
‘managing the health and wellbeing impact of new development’. The policy
requires all development proposals to be considered in relation to their
potential health, wellbeing and wider amenity impacts. It requires that
developments must not unreasonably harm the amenities of occupants and
neighbouring uses, including those of nearby residents. It cites some
particular matters that developments must avoid, the most relevant in this
case being: a) creating a sense of overbearing or oppressive enclosure and
b) unreasonably affecting the level of privacy to buildings and land that
owners and occupiers might expect to enjoy. There are similar
considerations contained in policy GD6(3).
61. There is a body of Jersey caselaw9 concerning how amenity assessments
should be made in planning determinations. Whilst these cases related to an
earlier Island Plan era, and policy GD1 has evolved somewhat in the
intervening period, there is nothing to suggest a departure from the main
principles that arise from those judgements. These include the recognition
that assessments are contextual and relative, and that in locations where
development is directed, i.e. the BUA, some harm from new developments
is to be expected. The key consideration for the decision maker is whether
any identified harm crosses the threshold of being ‘unreasonable’, which is
not defined, and is a matter for the decision maker.
62. I will deal with the overlooking and overbearing issues in turn.
Overlooking/privacy
63. It is a characteristic of the locality that the seaward facing dwellings along
La Cache du Bourg have a mix of windows, glazed doors, glazed structures
and some balconies, enjoying southerly views of the beach and sea. A
consequence of this is that the modest depth garden spaces between the
dwellings and the beach tend to be heavily overlooked by their neighbours.
This is particularly so from upstairs locations. On my site inspection, I made
observations from Beachcroft’s first floor glazed balcony, which is accessible
from 2 bedrooms; it projects outwards about 1.5 metres from the rear
elevation, has a span of over 6 metres and is glazed on 3 sides. From this
viewpoint, I could see most of the amenity space of Landfall and La Greve. I
could also look sideways and see secondary windows in the side wall of
Landfall, which projects a few metres further forward than Beachcroft.
64. The appeal proposal would not raise any overlooking concerns at ground
floor level, as there are screen walls. At first floor level, a balcony accessible
from bedroom 1 would afford views not dissimilar to those from the existing
glazed balcony, although the inclusion of flank walls would likely lessen
9 Boyle and Kehoe -v- Minister for Planning [2012] JRC036; Winchester -v- Minister for Planning and
Environment [2014] JRC118
11

sideways overlooking. The more notable change would be at the second-
floor balcony level, as this would introduce a new and more elevated
viewing position compared to the existing situation. This could be somewhat
more intrusive for the immediate neighbours. However, if privacy screens
were to be fitted on the west and east sides of the balcony, that would focus
balcony users’ views seawards and lessen any intrusion or perceived
intrusion. Such privacy screens could be secured by a planning condition,
and I note that this formed part of the original officer recommendation to
the Planning Committee.
65. I am satisfied that, subject to a planning condition requiring privacy screens
to be fixed and maintained to the second-floor balcony, there would be no
unreasonable loss of privacy effects, given the specific site context.
Physical impacts
66. The precise GD1 policy test here is whether the physical presence of the
proposal would create ‘a sense of overbearing or oppressive enclosure’ that
would be unreasonable. The Committee’s second reason makes specific
reference to height and proximity to the boundaries.
67. The proposed building would be notably bigger and bulkier than Beachcroft.
It would also be marginally taller, and its flat roof design means that there
will be more building mass at the upper level. This is clearly shown on
Section S-02 on drawing number 103 Rev A. The footprint would also be a
little wider and, whilst the narrow path width would be retained on its west
side, the gap on its east side would largely disappear, the new building
sitting relatively tight up to this boundary, with Le Greve.
68. However, most of the mass of the building would sit in the zone between
the neighbouring properties either side, and the inevitably ‘slabby’ side
elevations would be largely, but not fully, screened by the adjacent
buildings. The greater bulk would be more apparent in some views from the
neighbouring dwellings. In this regard I have looked carefully at the change
that the proposal would introduce and, whilst I can appreciate that the
appearance of more building mass from neighbours’ viewpoints might be
unwelcome, I do not assess it to be unreasonable in GD1 terms. I consider
that both neighbouring properties would still enjoy high standards of
amenity and would not be subject to unreasonable harm through a sense of
overbearing or oppressive enclosure.
Ground 2 conclusion
69. I do not consider that the proposal would cause unreasonable harm to the
living conditions of its neighbours. I find no conflict with policy GD1.
Ground 3 – Overall BIP compliance
70. The appellant’s third ground contends that when considered ‘in the round’,
the proposal satisfies the BIP policies and associated SPG. In this regard, I
note that the appellant’s Statement of Case cites claims of compliance with
a wide range of BIP policies including those relating to foul sewerage,
12

biodiversity and geodiversity, off street parking, housing quality and
residential space standards.
71. However, even accepting these claims, and my findings on ground 2
concerning policy GD1 and amenity matters, these are largely neutral
matters that all developments are expected to satisfy. My finding of conflict
with policy GD5, and a consequential conflict with policy SP1, are
fundamental, and, in my view, fatal to the scheme as submitted. This is
because, as I assessed above, policy GD5 plays an important, indeed pivotal
role, within the BIP’s wider development strategy and its strategic response
to climate change. The policy conflict is therefore significant and weighty,
and not outweighed by other considerations, in my assessment.
72. I assess that the proposal is not in accordance with the BIP and the ground
3 appeal should not succeed.
PLANNING CONDITIONS
73. At the Hearing, I held a ‘without prejudice’ discussion on planning
conditions. Should the Minister be minded not to accept my
recommendation and grant planning permission, the range of planning
conditions that appear in the original officer report would all meet the tests
of being necessary and reasonable.
74. Additionally, I also think that a condition preventing amenity use of the top
floor flat roofed area on the roadside of the dwelling would be prudent, in
the interests of safeguarding the amenities of occupants of neighbouring
properties.
CONCLUSIONS AND RECOMMENDATION
75. The appeal proposal to demolish the existing Beachcroft dwelling and
construct a replacement house has been carefully assessed against the BIP
policies.
76. I have found that whilst the proposed development would offer modern
design features and improved internal space for occupants, it would
fundamentally conflict with policy GD5, which prioritises the retention and
refurbishment of existing buildings, unless clear justification is provided
under one of its three specified tests.
77. The evidence presented fails to demonstrate that any of the three policy
GD5 tests, which would allow for demolition and replacement, have been
met. This means that the proposal presents a fundamental conflict with
policy GD5 and with policy SP1, which emphasizes climate change
mitigation through the reuse and retrofitting of existing structures.
78. I have assessed that the proposal would not result in unreasonable harm to
the amenities of neighbouring properties and that, subject to planning
condition requirements, policy GD1 could be satisfied. However, this does
not outweigh the significant policy conflicts identified under GD5 and SP1. I
have noted a range of other policy matters and material considerations, but
none of these outweigh the primary policy conflict that I have identified.
13

79. I recommend that the appeal should be DISMISSED, although I further
recommend that Reason 2 included in the Decision Notice should be deleted
in the light of my findings.
P. Staddon
Mr Philip Staddon BSc, Dip, MBA, MRTPI
14
location: Beachcroft, La Cache du Bourg, St. Clement
